coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-induced developmental toxicity in Daphnia magna involves disturbance of symbiotic microorganisms
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunfeng
Liu, Yanhua
Chen, Wenling
Li, Fei
Guo, Ruixin
Ji, Rong
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing synthesis and application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), their prevalence as pollution in water environments has increased. However, the toxic effects of CQDs on aquatic organisms are unclear, and their environmental safety must be evaluated. Herein, Daphnia magna was used as a model organism to explore the developmental toxicity of CQDs under a full life-cycle exposure. It was found that the feeding rate and offing number of D. magna decreased with increasing CQD concentration, and the body length of D. magna showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. These results indicated that long-term exposure to CQDs has evident toxic effects on D. magna development. Symbiosis analysis showed that the composition of the symbiotic microbial community of D. magna was disturbed by CQDs. The abundance of microorganisms involved in the immune response of D. magna such as Rhodobacter, decreased; those involved in the inflammation such as Gemmobacter, increased; and those involved in the nitrogen cycle, such as Hydrogenophaga and Paracoccus, decreased. When D. magna was subjected to environmental pressure, host-microflora interactive immune regulation was induced. The abundance of probiotics in D. magna, such as Rhodococcus, increased in response to environmental pressure. The results of KEGG function prediction showed that the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms involved in energy absorption and metabolism was affected by CQDs. In addition, the correlation analysis showed that there was a correlation between the changes in the symbiotic microbial community and the damage to D. magna after exposure to CQDs. Thus, it is appealed that as a potential environmental pollutant, CQDs have aquatic environmental risks, and their safe application deserves attention.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Daphnia magna
Full life-cycle exposure
Developmental toxicity
Symbiotic microorganisms
Aquatic ecological risk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Developmental toxicity of CQDs to D. magna
Effects of CQDs on the symbiotic microorganisms of D. magna
Diversity and composition of symbiotic microorganisms
Microbial communities with statistically significant differences
Functional prediction of daphnia's symbiotic bacteria
Relationship between physiological characteristics of D. magna and bacterial community

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22276216 , 22306203 ), Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province (2023), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities Research ( 2632022PY01 ), and College Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of different concentrations of CQDs on the feeding rate (A), body length (B) and total number of offspring (C) of D. magna. CK: blank group; LC: 0.15 mg/L CQDs; MC: 1.5 mg/L CQDs; HC: 15 mg/L CQDs. *p < 0.05 represents a significant difference from the control group.

Shannon index (A) and principal coordinate analysis (PCOA) (B) of symbiotic microorganisms in different treatments at the age of 30 days in D. magna. Distribution of the microbial community for each sample at the phylum (C) and genus (D) levels. CK: blank group; LC: 0.15 mg/L CQDs; MC: 1.5 mg/L CQDs; HC: 15 mg/L CQDs. The asterisk indicates a significant difference (**: p < 0.01; ***: p < 0.001).

Key phylotypes of the daphnia's symbiotic microorganisms responding to CQD exposure, as identified using linear discriminant analysis (LDA) effect size (LEfSe): LDA scores of the differentially abundant taxa of the CQD exposure group compared with the control group (Note: the higher the LDA score, the greater the contribution to the magnitude of the variation) (A). Cladograms based on the LEfSe analysis for the CQD groups compared with the control group (red and green circles indicate significant differences in the relative abundance; yellow circles indicate nonsignificant mean non-significant differences) (B).

Heatmap of dominant OTU levels (abundance >0.5 %) in different treatments (A). Microorganisms with significant differences between CK and LC (B), MC (C), and HC (D) at the OTU level. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

The predicted KEGG pathways at level 3 related to the lipid metabolism(A) and the endocrine system (B) based on the abundance of bacteria between the control and the CQD treatment groups. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.
Spearman correlation matrix of microorganisms (only the significantly changed microorganisms) with the body and total number of offspring of D. magna. * p < 0.05.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-induced developmental toxicity in Daphnia magna involves disturbance of symbiotic microorganisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, 210009 Nanjing, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yunfeng Ma and Yanhua Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing synthesis and application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), their prevalence as pollution in water environments has increased. However, the toxic effects of CQDs on aquatic organisms are unclear, and their environmental safety must be evaluated. Herein, Daphnia magna was used as a model organism to explore the developmental toxicity of CQDs under a full life-cycle exposure. It was found that the feeding rate and offing number of D. magna decreased with increasing CQD concentration, and the body length of D. magna showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. These results indicated that long-term exposure to CQDs has evident toxic effects on D. magna development. Symbiosis analysis showed that the composition of the symbiotic microbial community of D. magna was disturbed by CQDs. The abundance of microorganisms involved in the immune response of D. magna such as Rhodobacter, decreased; those involved in the inflammation such as Gemmobacter, increased; and those involved in the nitrogen cycle, such as Hydrogenophaga and Paracoccus, decreased. When D. magna was subjected to environmental pressure, host-microflora interactive immune regulation was induced. The abundance of probiotics in D. magna, such as Rhodococcus, increased in response to environmental pressure. The results of KEGG function prediction showed that the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms involved in energy absorption and metabolism was affected by CQDs. In addition, the correlation analysis showed that there was a correlation between the changes in the symbiotic microbial community and the damage to D. magna after exposure to CQDs. Thus, it is appealed that as a potential environmental pollutant, CQDs have aquatic environmental risks, and their safe application deserves attention.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials widely used in many fields such as photocatalysis, drug carriers and biological imaging due to their unique physical and chemical properties, such as high hydrophilicity, high cell permeability, high biocompatibility, and low biological toxicity, etc. (Qu et al., 2018; Caglayan et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). However, during their production and use, CQDs inevitably enter the aquatic environment (Bauerlein et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2022). At present, most of the research on CQDs has focused on their production and use, while few studies have focused on their aquatic ecological risk. A previous study reported that the survival rate of zebrafish embryos decreased to 55 % after 24 h of exposure to 2.5 mg/mL of CQDs (Kang et al., 2015). Exposure to CQDs resulted in morphological malformations in zebrafish and minnow embryos (Xiao et al., 2016). CQDs inhibited the photosynthesis of Skeletonema obliquus (Yao et al., 2018). Thus, CQDs have toxic effects on aquatic organisms, which must be systematically studied.

As an important zooplankton in aquatic environments, D. magna plays an important role in the aquatic ecosystems. Damage to the D. magna population caused by pollutants will directly or indirectly influences the balance of the aquatic ecosystem (Liu et al., 2020). In the natural environments, the impact of pollutants on D. magna is long-term and may affect the whole process of life cycle. Chronic exposure is more consistent with the actual situation. For example, Li et al. (2017) found that the whole-life-stage exposure to tris(1,3-dichloro-2-propyl) phosphate significantly decreased the survival rate of D. magna at the stage of the exposure experiment (>32 days), but did not significantly differ in the groups exposed to different concentrations when the exposure time was <32 days. Therefore, it is of great significance to analyze the full life-cycle toxicity of CQDs.

The symbiotic microbiota is one of the important factors in the relationship between an organism's body health and pathogenesis. The symbiotic microbiota of D. magna is involved in regulating a variety of physiological functions, and many diseases are related to changes in the composition of their symbiotic microbiota (Jin et al., 2017; Kanmani et al., 2020). Studies have found that CQDs can be used as broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents (Anand et al., 2019), and they have inhibitory effects on pathogenic bacteria such as Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella typhimurium (Kang and Kang, 2021). Chen et al. (2022), found that the abundance and diversity of carp intestinal microorganisms were disturbed by CQDs, leading to an increase in the relative abundance of harmful bacteria and a decrease in the relative abundance of beneficial bacteria. However, it is not known whether the exposure to CQDs affects the symbiotic microbiota of D. magna and disrupts their development.

Thus, the developmental toxicity of CQDs to D. magna under full life-cycle exposure was explored, and the toxic mechanism was investigated from the aspect of their influence on the symbiotic microbiota of D. magna. The three specific objectives of this study were: (1) to evaluate the developmental toxicity in D. magna based on full life-cycle CQD exposure; (2) to analyze the toxicity of CQDs on the symbiotic microbiota of D. magna; and (3) to explore the interaction between the development of D. magna and symbiotic microbiota. Through the above research, the aquatic environmental risks of CQDs were studied, and the function of symbiotic bacteria in ecotoxicology was elucidated.

CQDs were synthesized in our laboratory with glucose as the precursor, and the synthetic method and material characterization have been reported in our previous report (Liu et al., 2022). The details are shown in S1, Fig. S1 and S2 (supporting information). Transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai G2F20, USA) were used to observe the surface morphology. Briefly, the prepared CQDs were spherical in shape with good dispersion, and the average size was approximately 40 nm. The lattice fringes and diffraction rings of the CQDs were ambiguous, which indicated that the CQDs were amorphous (Fig. S2A). For the UV–vis (UV-2100, Shimadzu, Japan) absorption spectrum (blue line), one broad absorption band at approximately 273 nm was found, which was ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic bound CC. For fluorescence spectra, the optimum excitation (red line) and emission (black line) wavelengths were centered at 350 and 444 nm, respectively. The CQDs exhibited a bright yellowish-green color under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S2B). The surface functional groups present on the CQDs were identified by FTIR (IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu, Japan) data (Fig. S2C). The broad absorption bands in the 3100–3500 cm−1 range were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, and in this spectrum, stretching frequencies at 3382, 2928, 1695, 1611, 1284, and 1028 cm−1 were attributed to -OH (Zhang et al., 2004), CH (Victoria et al., 2020), CO (Victoria et al., 2020), CC (Sharma et al., 2017), C-O(Victoria et al., 2020), C-O-C bonds (Yadegari et al., 2019), respectively, while 1380 cm−1 was CH bending vibration (Lei et al., 2019). The elemental composition of CQDs was studied by XPS (Phi 5000, Kanagawa, Japan) characterization (Fig. S2D). Two sharp peaks at 284.58 and 532.53 eV were attributed to Cls and Ols, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cls (Fig. S2E) indicated the existence of C-C/C=C (284.58 eV), while that of Ols (Fig. S2F) indicated the existence of C-O-C/C-OH (532.53 eV). The above results demonstrated that these CQDs have high water solubility.

Chlorella pyrenoidosa was purchased from the Freshwater Algae Culture Collection at the Institute of Hydrobiology in China. C. pyrenoidosa was incubated in the BG-11 medium (Supplementary Tables S1 and S2) and were cultured in a light culture shaker with a light intensity of 2500 lx, a light: dark cycle of 16 h: 8 h, a temperature of 25 ± 1 °C and a speed of 120 rpm. D. magna was maintained in our laboratory and incubated with moderately hard water with a light: dark cycle of 16 h: 8 h, at a temperature of 22 ± 1 °C, the pH of 7.3–7.7, and conductivity of 300 ± 20 μs/cm, and the medium was renewed once every two days. C. pyrenoidosa was used as a food source at a density of 1 × 106 cells/mL.

CQD exposure experiments were conducted using 7-day-old D. magna. Each experimental condition comprised five biological replicates (n = 5) consisting of five 100 mL beakers. Each beaker contained five D. magna and 60 mL of exposure solution. The 0 (CK group), 0.15 (LC group), 1.5 (MC group), and 15 mg/L CQDs (HC group) were used for toxicity assay. The initial algal density was 1 × 106 cells/mL. All groups were maintained in the dark to prevent the growth of microalgae. The experiment was stopped when the survival rate of D. magna was as low as 75 % (47-d old). The medium was renewed every day and the offspring were counted and removed. The feeding rate (see part SII in the Supporting Information file), body length (see part SIII in the Supporting Information file), and number of offspring were recorded every day.

Under similar pollution levels and food concentration conditions, the system was expanded (including 200 D. magna in 1200 mL of exposure solution) for further analysis of the developmental toxicity mechanism of CQDs in D. magna. The symbiotic bacterial composition of D. magna was determined at the peak reproductive period (30-d old) (Li et al., 2017).

Thirty D. magna were used to determine the composition of symbiotic bacteria (n = 5). Total genomic DNA of the microbial community was extracted according to the instructions of the EZNA® soil DNA kit (Omega Biotek, Norcross, GA, US). The quality of the extracted genomic DNA was detected using 1 % agarose gel electrophoresis, and the concentration and purity of the DNA were determined using a NanoDrop2000 (Thermo Scientific, USA). PCR amplification was performed using extracted DNA as a template, and three replicates were performed for each sample. PCR products from the same sample were recovered using 2 % agarose gel, purified using the AxyPrep DNA Gel Extraction Kit (Axygen Biosciences, Union City, CA, USA), and quantified using a Quantus™ Fluorometer (Promega, USA). Three replicates were used for each group. Detailed data analysis of the sequencing is described in the SIV part in the Supporting Information file.

The SPSS statistical software package (version 26, SPSS Inc.) was used for statistical analyses, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to evaluate the significant differences among the groups, including feeding rate, body length, and number of offspring of D. magna. Levene's test was used to test homoscedasticity before significance analysis. *p < 0.05 and **p < 0.01 were set as the significance levels for all statistical tests. The RDP classifier (http://rdp.cme.msu.edu/,version2.11) was compared to the Silva 16S rRNA gene database (v138) to obtain OTU (operational taxonomic unit) taxonomic annotations (70 % confidence threshold). The Wilcoxon rank sum test, principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) of the Bray-Curtis distance algorithm, PERMANOVA nonparametric test, and LEfSe analysis (linear discriminant analysis effect size) (http://huttenhower. sph.harvard.edu/LEfSe) (LDA > 2, p < 0.05)were used to conduct statistical analysis of the symbiotic bacteria of D. magna.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developmental toxicity of CQDs to D. magna
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the toxic effects of CQDs on D. magna, the development index, including feeding rate, body length and offspring number was studied. As shown in Fig. 1A, the feeding rates of D. magna (30-d old: the peak reproductive period) in CK (0 mg/L CQDs), LC (0.15 mg/L CQDs), MC (1.5 mg/L CQDs) and HC (15 mg/L CQDs) groups were (0.29 ± 0.04) × 106 (mean ± standard deviation), (0.34 ± 0.05) × 106, (0.26 ± 0.01) × 106 and (0.06 ± 0.01) × 106 cell/ind./h, respectively. Compared with the control group, the feeding rate of D. magna was higher in the LC exposure group, and the other groups were lower. The feeding rates of D. magna (47-d old) in the CK, LC, MC and HC groups were (0.28 ± 0.01) × 106, (0.25 ± 0.02) × 106, (0.18 ± 0.01) × 106 and (0.04 ± 0.01) × 106 cell/ind./h, respectively. Compared with the control group, the feeding rate decreased by 10.71 %, 35.71 % and 85.71 %, respectively. In general, the feeding rate of D. magna in the MC and HC groups was significantly lower than that in the control group, indicating that CQDs affected the feeding behavior of D. magna in a concentration-time dependence. The body length of D. magna was affected by CQDs at three different concentrations (Fig. 1B). Compared with the control group, the body length of D. magna in the LC group was stimulated, with stimulation rates of 3.8 %. Meanwhile, the body length was inhibited in the MC group, with inhibition rates of 3.1 %. However, there was no significant difference in body length between the HC group and the control group. This might be due to the impact of exposure to 15 mg/L CQDs on energy reserves provoked reduced reproduction, thus the impact was too high to be balanced by redistribution toward reproduction (Annika et al., 2013). CQDs exposure changed the reproductive behavior of D. magna (Fig. S2), and CQDs exposure inhibited the total number of offspring, which decreased by 5.0 % (0.15 mg/L CQDs), 6.8 % (1.5 mg/L CQDs) and 13.7 % (15 mg/L CQDs, p < 0.05), respectively (Fig. 1C).

The feeding mode of D. magna is filtration, which does not distinguish food types during the feeding process. The size of CQDs is approximately 40 nm, which is smaller than that of C. pyrenoidosa (5–10 μm) (Pan et al., 2015). Therefore, CQDs can enter the intestine of D. magna through filtration. Compared with the control group, the lower feeding rate of D. magna in the medium and high concentration groups may have been related to the ratio of CQDs/Chlorella pyrenoidosa. The amount of Chlorella pyrenoidosa ingested by D. magna decreased as the proportion increased. Consistent with the results of Ogonowski et al. (2016) and Kelpsiene et al. (2020), the feeding rate of D. magna decreased with increasing microplastic concentration. However, a previous study reported that under the pressure of pollutants, more energy is allocated to detoxification and recovery, and less energy is allocated to feeding, growth and reproduction (Issa et al., 2021). In this study, the feeding rate of D. magna decreased after exposure to CQDs, resulting in a reduction in energy for the growth and reproduction of D. magna.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on the symbiotic microorganisms of D. magna
Diversity and composition of symbiotic microorganisms
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the Fig. 2A, the symbiotic microbiota of D. magna was disrupted after CQD exposure. Alpha diversity analysis showed that the Shannon indices of the MC group were significantly higher than those of the CK group, indicating that exposure to 1.5 mg/L of CQDs increased the symbiotic microbial community diversity of D. magna. However, the results of the HC group were opposite, i.e., 15 mg/L of CQDs decreased the daphnia's symbiotic microbial community diversity. Principal coordinate analysis (PCOA) also indicated that 15 mg/L of CQDs significantly changed the composition of the symbiotic microbial community in D. magna (Fig. 2B). The diversity of the symbiotic microbial community is an important indicator of host health. For example, compared with good food conditions, the microbial diversity of D. magna was significantly reduced under highly toxic food conditions (Akbar et al., 2021a). The intermediate disturbance hypothesis (IDH) suggests that microbial diversity is the highest when an ecosystem is moderately disturbed. When the ecosystem is seriously damaged, microbial diversity is reduced, and only species with a strong adaptation ability can survive (Feswick et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013; Swart et al., 2019). In this study, the abundance of daphnia symbiotic microorganisms increased after 1.5 mg/L CQDs exposure, but significantly decreased after 15 mg/L CQDs exposure. The changes in the microbial community caused by CQDs are in accordance with the IDH.

In the complex symbiotic relationship between animals and microorganisms, beneficial bacteria can provide many benefits to the host, such as resisting the invasion of intestinal pathogens, increasing the host's absorption of nutrients, and participating in the host's immune system function (Nishida et al., 2018). The biological health and composition of symbiotic microorganisms are affected by the environment. Proteobacteria and Actinobacteriota were the top two dominant phyla in abundance in the CQD exposure groups, accounting for >95 % of the total bacterial abundance (Fig. 2C). Similar results were also observed in the other studies (Sangkuanun et al., 2020; Akbar et al., 2021b). Organic substances such as environment-derived fatty acids, aromatics, carbohydrates, and peptides could be used by the abovementioned bacteria with multiple functions (Zhou et al., 2022), thus increasing the ability of D. magna to absorb nutrients and adapt to the environment. Hydrogenophaga, Paracoccus and Acinetobacter were the top three dominant families in the CK (0 mg/L CQDs) group. Acinetobacter, Unclassified-f-Rhodobacteraceae and Rhodococcus were the top three dominant families in the LC (0.15 mg/L CQDs) group. Acinetobacter, Allorhizobium-Neorhizobium-Pararhizobium-Rhizoblum and Unclassified-f- Rhodobacteraceae were the top three dominant families in the MC (1.5 mg/L CQDs) group. Gemmobacter, Acinetobacter and Rhodococcus were the top three dominant families in the HC (15 mg/L CQDs) group (Fig. 2D). The changes in microbial composition at the genus level indicated that the relationship between D. magna and symbiotic microorganisms was disturbed after exposure to CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial communities with statistically significant differences
ce:section.ce:para: Linear discriminant analysis effect size (LEfSe) analyses were used to determine enriched biomarker taxa following CQD exposure. The LEfSe algorithm was used to statistically analyze 34 significantly different taxa from the phylum to genus levels (Fig. 3 ). To show the differences in microbes more intuitively, microbial communities with relative abundance >0.5 % were selected for significance analysis. The results showed that the relative abundance of Proteobacteria tended to decline at the phylum level after exposure to CQDs, and significantly decreased after exposure to 1.5 mg/L CQDs (Fig. S3). It was also found that the abundance of Proteobacteria in the intestine of common carp decreased after exposure to CQDs (Chen et al., 2022). A previous study reported that Proteobacteria is a phylum related to nitrogen cycling (Tan et al., 2020). The decrease in the abundance of Proteobacteria might be related to the reduction in the nitrogen cycles of daphnia's symbiotic microorganisms caused by CQDs. The relative abundance of Actinobaciota tended to increase and significantly increased after exposure to 0.15 and 1.5 mg/L CQDs (Fig. 2A–B). Rhodococcus is one of the genera of Actinobaciota (relative abundance >99 %), which belongs to probiotics and produces bioactive substances, such as antibiotics, organic acids, vitamins, and immunomodulatory substances (Yu et al., 2016). Host-microflora interactive immune regulation was triggered to deal with environmental pressure (Hicks et al., 2017). The increase in the abundance of Rhodococcus might be related to the regulatory function of enhancing the immune response of D. magna to resist the harsh environment. Our previous study found that CQDs increased intestinal inflammation in D. magna, and the intestinal inflammation increased with increasing concentrations of CQDs (Liu et al., 2022).

At the genus level, the relative abundance of Gemmobacter increased significantly in the CQD exposure groups, while the relative abundance of Hydrogenophaga, Paracocus and Ochrobactrum decreased significantly (Fig. S4). At the OTU level, the relative abundances of OTU281 (g_Gemmobacter) and OTU254 (g_Exiguobacterium) significantly increased in the CQD exposure groups, while the relative abundances of OTU211 (g_Paracoccus), OTU91 (_g_Hydrogenopaga), OTU160 (g_Ochrobactrum) and OTU126 (g_Rhodobacter) significantly decreased (Fig. 4 ). The current research on Gemmobacter has focused on the degradation of pollutants (Liu et al., 2020), but there have been few studies on the relationship between Gemmobacter as a symbiotic bacteria and hosts. Wu et al. (2022) found that the intestinal inflammation in crucian carp was positively correlated with the relative abundance of Gemmobacter after exposure to deltamethrin. Zhang et al. (2022) found that the combined exposure of aging microplastics and roxithromycin increased the intestinal inflammation of crucian carp and the relative abundance of Gemmobacter. In this study, the significant increase in Gemmobacter abundance might also be related to the inflammation of D. magna after exposure to CQDs (Liu et al., 2022). OTU126 (g__Rhodobacter) is a probiotic and contributes to increasing immunity by secreting lysogens (Liu et al., 2012). Chen et al. (2022) found that Rhodobacter was negatively correlated with immune inflammation parameters. Thus, the significant decrease in Rhodobacter abundance might be related to the decreased immunity and increased inflammation of D. magna. Hydrogenophaga is a kind of aerobic or facultatively anaerobic chemoautotroph that can conduct hydrogen oxidation without organic carbon and has denitrification (Zaitseva et al., 2014). Paracoccus belongs to denitrifying bacteria (Olaya-Abril et al., 2022). The feeding rate is positively correlated with the abundance of denitrifying bacteria (Hydrogenophaga and Paracoccus), which might be related to the decrease in the production and excretion of biological small molecular organic nitrogen, leading to a decrease in inorganic nitrogen content in the symbiotic system (Yin et al., 2023).

In general, CQDs disrupted the diversity of the symbiotic microbial community in daphnia under life-cycle exposure, and the symbiotic microbial abundance at the level of phylum, genus and OTU levels was significantly affected. On the one hand, the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms related to the immunity of D. magna decreased, such as Gemmobacter and Rhodobacter, and the abundance of microorganisms related to the nitrogen cycle increased, such as Hydrogenophaga and Paracoccus, which might be related to the intestinal inflammation of D. magna after exposure to CQDs. On the other hand, the host-microflora interactive immune regulation was triggered and the relative abundance of probiotics increased (e.g., Rhodococcus) to deal with the environmental pressure caused by CQD exposure. The above results indicated that the diversity and composition of symbiotic microorganisms were affected by environmental pollutants, and then further affected the health of the host.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional prediction of daphnia's symbiotic bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Further functional analysis based on the KEGG annotations was further carried out (Figs. 5 , S5, S6). The KEGG pathways related to lipid metabolism and endocrine were enriched. Sixteen pathways participate in lipid metabolism among which the abundance of OTU bacteria associated with ‘glyceroolipid metabolism’ and ‘secondary bile acid biosynthesis’ decreased with the increasing of CQD concentrations, and there was a significant difference between the HC group and the control group. In addition, 18 pathways involved in the endocrine system were identified. Among them, the OTU levels of bacteria related to the ‘GnRH signaling pathway’, ‘PPAR signaling pathway’, ‘adipocytokine signaling pathway’, ‘estrogen signaling pathway’, ‘progesterone-mediated oocyte formation’, ‘renin-angiotensin system’ and ‘renin secret’ decreased with the increasing of CQDs concentration, and there was significant difference between the HC group and the control group.

The balance between energy consumption and intake is very important for the normal development of organisms. When the energy of D. magna decreases, stored energy is preferentially used, such as sugar and triacylglycerol (Ahmadian et al., 2007). Lipids play a functional and structural role in biological processes, such as energy supply, biological membranes (e.g., phospholipid bilayer) and signal molecules (e.g., hormones). Lipid metabolism is crucial to species survival and reproduction because it is the basic cellular component for animals to maintain homeostasis under environmental pressure (Lee et al., 2018). Herein, D. magna was in a poor nutritional status induced by CQDs. To maintain life activities, the hydrolysis of stored energy increases in exposed D. magna, such as the hydrolysis of triacylglycerol, and in the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms involved in lipid metabolism further increases. At the same time, when most lipids were used for energy supply, the energy for growth was relatively reduced; for example, the body length of D. magna decreased. Lipids related to signaling molecules and biomembrane formation were decreased, such as ‘gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH)’ and ‘estrogen and progesterone’. Embryonic development is regulated by the progesterone and estrogen in D. magna (Navis et al., 2018). The decrease in the synthesis of hormones related to embryonic development might lead to a decrease in the number of offsping of D. magna (Sumiya et al., 2016), and might also induce a decrease in the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms involved in signaling molecules and biofilms.

In general, the results of functional analysis based on the KEGG showed that the abundance of daphnia symbiotic microorganisms involved in energy metabolism changed under a full life-cycle exposure. Specifically, under environmental pressure, the energy involved in life maintenance activities increased in the host (lipid metabolism) and induced an increase in the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms related to fat metabolism. The energy involved in the growth and reproduction of D. magna was decreased and induced a decrease in the lipid content related to signal molecules and biofilm formation, resulting in a decrease in the abundance of endocrine related symbiotic bacteria, the body length of F1 and the number of offspring of D. magna.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between physiological characteristics of D. magna and bacterial community
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential relationship between the physiological characteristics and symbiotic microorganisms in exposed D. magna, a correlation heatmap between the physiological characteristics and symbiotic microorganisms and microbial function prediction was generated by Spearman correlation analysis (Fig. 6 ). Three symbiotic microorganisms at the genera level were studied by Spearman correlation analysis, including Paracoccus，Ochrobactrum and Gemmobacter. Among them, Paracoccus, OTU211 (g__Paracoccus), Ochrobactrum and OTU160 (g__Ochrobactrum) were negatively associated with the number of offspring (Fig. 6). Ochrobactrum can degrade plant fiber by producing highly active cellulase and hemicellulase, and can also synthesize vitamin B (Yang et al., 2021). Vitamin B12 is necessary for the normal reproduction of D. magna (Keating, 1985). The decrease in the abundance of Ochrobactrum might reduce the content of highly active enzymes and vitamin B used for the intestinal digestion of D. magna and reduce the nutrients in the process of oocyte development, resulting in the reduction of the body length and the number of offspring of exposed D. magna.

Associations between the physiological characteristics and symbiotic microorganisms were calculated by Spearman correlation analysis. The results showed that 360 functional pathways at level 3 were described (Table S3). Among them, 69 functional pathways were correlated with the number of offspring of D. magna, and 55 functional pathways were positively correlated with the number of offspring. These functional pathways mainly included signal transduction (6) and endocrine related functional pathways (6). Twelve functional pathways were negatively correlated with the number of offspring, which mainly included carbohydrate metabolism. Five functional pathways were positively correlated with the body length of D. magna. The number of offspring is significantly positively correlated with the pathway related to the oocyte development. The GnRH signaling pathway could activate intracellular calcium signaling pathways and stimulate calcium release (Wu et al., 2009), which is crucial during folliculogenesis (Vegetti and Alagna, 2006). Ge et al. (2022) showed that the spawning behavior of zebrafish under low temperature was controlled by the GnRH signaling pathway. In this study, the decrease in the number of offspring of D. magna decreased might be related to the decrease in the abundance of symbiotic microorganisms related to egg cell development.

The above results revealed the possible interaction between symbiotic microorganisms and physiological damage to D. magna after CQD exposure. That is, the inhibition of the growth and development of D. magna induced by CQDs was related to the change in the symbiotic bacterial community structure.

The current research found that, in general, the feeding rate, growth and reproduction of D. magna were affected by CQD exposure, resulting in a lower feeding rate, a longer or shorter body length, and a fewer offspring. The feeding behavior of D. magna was inhibited after CQD exposure, resulting in energy absorption and metabolic disorders in D. magna. That is, more energy was used to maintain the normal life activities of D. magna and the energy for reproduction decreased, which was manifested by a significant reduction in the number of offspring of D. magna.

In the relationship with host-symbiotic bacteria, accompanied by low energy intake, D. magna could not provide sufficient nutrition for symbiotic microorganisms; at the same time, CQDs could cause intestinal inflammation in D. magna to induce changes in the composition of symbiotic microorganisms. The abundance of microorganisms related to the immunity of D. magna, such as Rhodobacter, decreased, and the abundance of microorganisms related to inflammation, such as Gemmobacter, increased. The abundance of microorganisms related to the nitrogen cycles of D. magna, such as Hydrogenophaga and Paracoccus, decreased. The host-microflora interactive immune regulation was triggered and increased the relative abundance of probiotics, such as Rhodococcus, to deal with the environmental pressure after exposure to CQDs. The results of functional analysis based on the KEGG showed that the abundance of microorganisms involved in energy metabolism changed under a full life-cycle CQD exposure. The energy involved in life maintenance activities increased in the host (lipid metabolism), and the energy involved in the growth and reproduction of D. magna decreased. Those factors might induce a decrease in the content of lipids related to signaling molecules and biofilm formation. In addition, correlation analysis showed that there was a significant correlation between the changes in the symbiotic microbial community and the physiological damage to D. magna after CQD exposure. Therefore, when the living environment changes, the impact of changes in symbiotic microorganisms on biological health is worth considering. This research provides new insights regarding the potential mechanism of aquatic toxicity caused by CQDs.
Yunfeng Ma & Yanhua Liu: carried out experiments and wrote the manuscript;
Wenling Chen & Fei Li: developed the methodology;
Ruixin Guo & Rong Ji: analyzed experimental results;
Jianqiu Chen: managed and coordinated responsibility for the research activity planning and execution.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22276216, 22306203), Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province (2023), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities Research (2632022PY01), and College Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.166825.
